1:
"The LORD also spake unto Joshua, saying," The witnesses note the LORD again speaking to Joshua, now concerning the cities of refuge. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord spoke out of the tabernacle, having before directed the dividing of the land, and now speaks again. The witnesses note that Henry notes many things were ordered by the law of Moses to be done when they came to Canaan, now to be performed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — from the tabernacle; the to Joshua — now again; the the land divided — mercy follows; the a refuge provided — a very present help in trouble (Ps. 46:1).

The witnesses agree the LORD himself initiates the appointing of the cities of refuge, speaking to Joshua from the tabernacle: now that the land was divided, God turns to provide for the manslayer a place of safety; the care that the gift of God should be followed by his merciful provision shows that he thinks on the needs of the weak and the endangered among his people; Henry marks that what the law of Moses had ordered was now to be fulfilled; it teaches that God is mindful of mercy as well as of inheritance, providing a refuge before the need arises, the same God who gives the land making a shelter for the distressed, "a very present help in trouble" (Ps. 46:1), the LORD speaking to appoint the refuge.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD spake: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: from the tabernacle. The witnesses note the to Joshua: now again. The witnesses note the the land divided: mercy follows. The witnesses note the a refuge provided: a very present help in trouble (Ps. 46:1). The expositors observe the the LORD spake: from the tabernacle. The expositors observe the to Joshua: now again. The expositors observe the the land divided: mercy follows. The expositors observe the a refuge provided: a very present help in trouble (Ps. 46:1). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the to Joshua, the the land divided, the a refuge provided, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the LORD spoke to Joshua to appoint the refuge — God mindful of mercy as of inheritance, providing a shelter before the need arises, a very present help in trouble (Ps. 46:1).
2:
"Speak to the children of Israel, saying, Appoint out for you cities of refuge, whereof I spake unto you by the hand of Moses:" The witnesses note the command to appoint cities of refuge, as foretold by Moses. The witnesses note that Gill notes the affair concerned the children of Israel, now having the whole land divided among them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command to appoint cities of refuge whereof God spake by Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the appoint cities of refuge — the command; the whereof I spake — by Moses; the a mercy of old — now performed; the a shadow of Christ — fled for refuge to lay hold on the hope (Heb. 6:18).

The witnesses agree the cities of refuge were no new device but the fulfilling of a word long before given "by the hand of Moses" (Num. 35:11): God had purposed this mercy from of old, and now brought it to pass; the appointing of cities set apart for the endangered shows the tender provision of God for the guilty-yet-not-malicious, a shelter from the avenger; the whole institution stands as a shadow of the refuge provided in Christ from before the world, the hope long promised and now revealed; it teaches that God's mercies are purposed of old and surely performed, "fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us" (Heb. 6:18), the cities of refuge appointed as Moses had foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse appoint cities of refuge: The witnesses note the appoint cities of refuge: the command. The witnesses note the whereof I spake: by Moses. The witnesses note the a mercy of old: now performed. The witnesses note the a shadow of Christ: fled for refuge to lay hold on the hope (Heb. 6:18). The expositors observe the appoint cities of refuge: the command. The expositors observe the whereof I spake: by Moses. The expositors observe the a mercy of old: now performed. The expositors observe the a shadow of Christ: fled for refuge to lay hold on the hope (Heb. 6:18). The believer is taught to mark the appoint cities of refuge, the whereof I spake, the a mercy of old, the a shadow of Christ, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that God commanded the cities of refuge, as foretold by Moses — his mercy purposed of old and surely performed, a shadow of the refuge in Christ, fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us (Heb. 6:18).
3:
"That the slayer that killeth any person unawares and unwittingly may flee thither: and they shall be your refuge from the avenger of blood." The witnesses note the purpose — that the unintentional slayer may flee there from the avenger of blood. The witnesses note that Gill notes one who through mere accident, without design or malice, killed a person, friend or foe, kindred or stranger. The witnesses note that Wesley notes "unwittingly" means through ignorance, error, or mistake. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the slayer unawares — not the murderer; the unwittingly — through ignorance (Wesley); the may flee thither — to the city; the a refuge from the avenger — fled for refuge, strong consolation (Heb. 6:18).

The witnesses agree the refuge was for him "that killeth any person unawares and unwittingly" — not the murderer, but the one who slew by accident, without malice: for such, the city was "a refuge from the avenger of blood"; the careful distinction between the wilful and the unwitting shows God's justice, that the malicious found no shelter, while the unintentional was spared; the fleeing slayer is a figure of the sinner who, made sensible of guilt and pursued by the law's avenging demand, flees to Christ for refuge; it teaches that there is a sure shelter for the guilty who flee to God's appointed place, even as the slayer, "we might have a strong consolation, who have fled for refuge" (Heb. 6:18), the city a refuge from the avenger.

So the witnesses make the verse a refuge from the avenger: The witnesses note the the slayer unawares: not the murderer. The witnesses note the unwittingly: through ignorance (Wesley). The witnesses note the may flee thither: to the city. The witnesses note the a refuge from the avenger: fled for refuge, strong consolation (Heb. 6:18). The expositors observe the the slayer unawares: not the murderer. The expositors observe the unwittingly: through ignorance (Wesley). The expositors observe the may flee thither: to the city. The expositors observe the a refuge from the avenger: fled for refuge, strong consolation (Heb. 6:18). The believer is taught to mark the the slayer unawares, the unwittingly, the may flee thither, the a refuge from the avenger, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the unintentional slayer might flee to the city from the avenger of blood — God's justice distinguishing the wilful from the unwitting, the fleeing slayer a figure of the sinner fleeing to Christ, who have fled for refuge (Heb. 6:18).
4:
"And when he that doth flee unto one of those cities shall stand at the entering of the gate of the city, and shall declare his cause in the ears of the elders of that city, they shall take him into the city unto them, and give him a place, that he may dwell among them." The witnesses note the slayer standing at the gate, declaring his cause, and being received into the city. The witnesses note that Gill notes he flees to the city nearest him. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the gate, where the judges sat; he gives a true relation of the fact, and if satisfied they take him in. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stand at the gate — the place of the elders; the declare his cause — his true account; the they take him in — received; the a place to dwell — him that cometh I will in no wise cast out (John 6:37).

The witnesses agree the fleeing slayer was received upon coming to the gate and "declaring his cause in the ears of the elders," who then took him in and gave him a place to dwell: the open reception of the one who came confessing his case shows the readiness of the refuge to all who sought it; Wesley notes the gate was the place of the judges, where his account was heard; the slayer found admittance and a dwelling within; it teaches that Christ receives all who come to him owning their need, refusing none who flee to him for shelter, "him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out" (John 6:37), the slayer taken in and given a place to dwell.

So the witnesses make the verse received at the gate: The witnesses note the stand at the gate: the place of the elders. The witnesses note the declare his cause: his true account. The witnesses note the they take him in: received. The witnesses note the a place to dwell: him that cometh I will in no wise cast out (John 6:37). The expositors observe the stand at the gate: the place of the elders. The expositors observe the declare his cause: his true account. The expositors observe the they take him in: received. The expositors observe the a place to dwell: him that cometh I will in no wise cast out (John 6:37). The believer is taught to mark the stand at the gate, the declare his cause, the they take him in, the a place to dwell, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the slayer was received at the gate and given a place to dwell — the refuge ready to all who came confessing, Christ receiving all who flee to him, him that cometh I will in no wise cast out (John 6:37).
5:
"And if the avenger of blood pursue after him, then they shall not deliver the slayer up into his hand; because he smote his neighbour unwittingly, and hated him not beforetime." The witnesses note the slayer protected from the avenger, since he killed without hatred. The witnesses note that Gill notes the avenger of blood might pursue him to the city and demand him. The witnesses note that the slayer hated him not beforetime, and smote him unwittingly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the avenger pursue — to the city; the not deliver him up — the slayer safe; the unwittingly — hated him not; the no condemnation — no condemnation to them in Christ (Rom. 8:1).

The witnesses agree the city gave sure protection — "they shall not deliver the slayer up into his hand" — because the killing was "unwittingly, and hated him not beforetime": once within the refuge, the manslayer was safe, the avenger forbidden to touch him; the security of the one who reached the city shows the perfect safety of those sheltered in God's appointed place; the believer who has fled to Christ is safe from every demand of the avenging law, kept beyond the reach of condemnation; it teaches that there is no condemnation to those who are in the refuge, "there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus" (Rom. 8:1), the slayer not delivered to the avenger.

So the witnesses make the verse not delivered up: The witnesses note the the avenger pursue: to the city. The witnesses note the not deliver him up: the slayer safe. The witnesses note the unwittingly: hated him not. The witnesses note the no condemnation: no condemnation to them in Christ (Rom. 8:1). The expositors observe the the avenger pursue: to the city. The expositors observe the not deliver him up: the slayer safe. The expositors observe the unwittingly: hated him not. The expositors observe the no condemnation: no condemnation to them in Christ (Rom. 8:1). The believer is taught to mark the the avenger pursue, the not deliver him up, the unwittingly, the no condemnation, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the slayer within the city was not delivered to the avenger — the perfect safety of those sheltered in God's refuge, the believer beyond the reach of the avenging law, no condemnation to them in Christ Jesus (Rom. 8:1).
6:
"And he shall dwell in that city, until he stand before the congregation for judgment, and until the death of the high priest that shall be in those days: then shall the slayer return... unto his own city..." The witnesses note the slayer dwelling in the city until judgment and until the death of the high priest, then returning home. The witnesses note that Gill notes he dwelt there until his cause was heard, and if found guilty only of accidental manslaughter, was kept safe until the high priest's death. The witnesses note that then he might return to his own city. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the dwell in the city — until judgment; the until the death of the high priest — then freed; the return to his own city — his liberty; the freed by the High Priest's death — that they might receive the inheritance (Heb. 9:15).

The witnesses agree the slayer's liberty was bound to "the death of the high priest that shall be in those days" — only at the high priest's death might he return freely home: this is the crowning type of the chapter, for it is by the death of our great High Priest, Jesus Christ, that the believer is set free and restored to his inheritance; the manslayer's release at the death of the priest foreshadows the liberty and homecoming purchased by Christ's dying; it teaches that the death of the High Priest opens the way of full freedom, the believer's release secured by the death of him who is "an high priest for ever" (Heb. 6:20), through whose death "they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance" (Heb. 9:15), the slayer freed at the high priest's death.

So the witnesses make the verse the death of the high priest: The witnesses note the dwell in the city: until judgment. The witnesses note the until the death of the high priest: then freed. The witnesses note the return to his own city: his liberty. The witnesses note the freed by the High Priest's death: that they might receive the inheritance (Heb. 9:15). The expositors observe the dwell in the city: until judgment. The expositors observe the until the death of the high priest: then freed. The expositors observe the return to his own city: his liberty. The expositors observe the freed by the High Priest's death: that they might receive the inheritance (Heb. 9:15). The believer is taught to mark the dwell in the city, the until the death of the high priest, the return to his own city, the freed by the High Priest's death, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the slayer was freed at the death of the high priest, then returned home — the crowning type, the believer set free and restored by the death of our great High Priest, that they which are called might receive the eternal inheritance (Heb. 9:15).
7:
"And they appointed Kedesh in Galilee in mount Naphtali, and Shechem in mount Ephraim, and Kirjatharba, which is Hebron, in the mountain of Judah." The witnesses note the three western cities of refuge — Kedesh, Shechem, and Hebron. The witnesses note that Gill notes the appointment of these three cities was made by the children of Israel at this time. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Kedesh in Galilee, in the north, among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Kedesh — holiness, in the north; the Shechem — the shoulder, in the centre; the Hebron — fellowship, in the south; the near to all — not far from every one of us (Acts 17:27).

The witnesses agree the three western cities — "Kedesh... Shechem... Hebron" — were set in the north, the centre, and the south, that a refuge might be near to all: the spreading of the cities through the land shows that the shelter was made accessible to every part, none too far to flee; the very names breathe of the gospel — Kedesh, "holiness"; Shechem, "the shoulder" that bears; Hebron, "fellowship" — fit figures of the Christ who is our sanctuary, our burden-bearer, and our communion; it teaches that God set his refuge within reach of all his people, as Christ is "not far from every one of us" (Acts 17:27), the three cities appointed through the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the western cities: The witnesses note the Kedesh: holiness, in the north. The witnesses note the Shechem: the shoulder, in the centre. The witnesses note the Hebron: fellowship, in the south. The witnesses note the near to all: not far from every one of us (Acts 17:27). The expositors observe the Kedesh: holiness, in the north. The expositors observe the Shechem: the shoulder, in the centre. The expositors observe the Hebron: fellowship, in the south. The expositors observe the near to all: not far from every one of us (Acts 17:27). The believer is taught to mark the Kedesh, the Shechem, the Hebron, the near to all, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that Kedesh, Shechem, and Hebron were appointed through the land — the refuge made accessible to all, their names figures of Christ our sanctuary, burden-bearer, and fellowship, not far from every one of us (Acts 17:27).
8:
"And on the other side Jordan by Jericho eastward, they assigned Bezer in the wilderness upon the plain out of the tribe of Reuben, and Ramoth in Gilead out of the tribe of Gad, and Golan in Bashan out of the tribe of Manasseh." The witnesses note the three eastern cities of refuge — Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were in the country possessed by Reuben, Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Bezer, Ramoth in Gilead, and Golan in Bashan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Bezer — a fortress; the Ramoth — the heights; the Golan — joy; the a refuge on every side — the name of the LORD a strong tower (Prov. 18:10).

The witnesses agree three more cities were set "on the other side Jordan" — "Bezer... Ramoth... Golan" — that the eastern tribes too might have a refuge near: the providing of cities on both sides of Jordan shows that none of God's people, however placed, were left without a shelter within reach; the names again are rich — Bezer, "a stronghold" or "fortress"; Ramoth, "the heights" or "exalted"; Golan, "joy" — figures of the Christ who is our fortress, our exaltation, and our joy; it teaches that the refuge of God is provided for all his people alike, on every side, the stronghold of the LORD their high tower, "the name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe" (Prov. 18:10), the three eastern cities assigned.

So the witnesses make the verse the eastern cities: The witnesses note the Bezer: a fortress. The witnesses note the Ramoth: the heights. The witnesses note the Golan: joy. The witnesses note the a refuge on every side: the name of the LORD a strong tower (Prov. 18:10). The expositors observe the Bezer: a fortress. The expositors observe the Ramoth: the heights. The expositors observe the Golan: joy. The expositors observe the a refuge on every side: the name of the LORD a strong tower (Prov. 18:10). The believer is taught to mark the Bezer, the Ramoth, the Golan, the a refuge on every side, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan were assigned east of Jordan — the refuge provided for all God's people on every side, their names figures of Christ our fortress, exaltation, and joy, the name of the LORD a strong tower (Prov. 18:10).
9:
"These were the cities appointed for all the children of Israel, and for the stranger that sojourneth among them, that whosoever killeth any person at unawares might flee thither..." The witnesses note the cities appointed for all Israel and for the stranger, that any unintentional slayer might flee and not die before judgment. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were for the common use of all, not for that tribe only in which they stood. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note they were for all the children of Israel and for the stranger that sojourned among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for all Israel — the common refuge; the and the stranger — the Gentile included; the whosoever killeth unawares — might flee; the the whosoever of grace — rich unto all that call on him (Rom. 10:12).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by declaring the refuge open to "all the children of Israel, and for the stranger that sojourneth among them": the cities were for the common use of all, and — most graciously — the stranger, the Gentile sojourner, was included; the throwing-open of the refuge to the foreigner foreshadows the gospel grace that gathers Jew and Gentile alike into the one shelter of Christ; it teaches that God's refuge is for all who flee to it, of every nation, none shut out who come, "the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him" (Rom. 10:12); thus the cities of refuge proclaim the whosoever of the gospel, "whosoever... might flee thither," the shelter open to all who come, even the stranger.

So the witnesses make the verse for all and the stranger: The witnesses note the for all Israel: the common refuge. The witnesses note the and the stranger: the Gentile included. The witnesses note the whosoever killeth unawares: might flee. The witnesses note the the whosoever of grace: rich unto all that call on him (Rom. 10:12). The expositors observe the for all Israel: the common refuge. The expositors observe the and the stranger: the Gentile included. The expositors observe the whosoever killeth unawares: might flee. The expositors observe the the whosoever of grace: rich unto all that call on him (Rom. 10:12). The believer is taught to mark the for all Israel, the and the stranger, the whosoever killeth unawares, the the whosoever of grace, and to flee for refuge to Christ. The verse thus records that the cities were for all Israel and the stranger, that whosoever fled might be safe — the refuge open to Jew and Gentile alike, the whosoever of the gospel, the same Lord rich unto all that call upon him (Rom. 10:12).
